coredata.dc:title: Boosted charge transfer in p-n heterojunctions LaMoO3/GQDs negative electrode for all-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Zijun
Liu, Juyin
Gao, Yanfang
Li, Lijun
Cao, Zhenzhu
coredata.dc:description: The low energy density of asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) is limited by the negative electrodes; thus, to further increase the energy density of ASCs, it is necessary to optimize the design of the negative electrode and the device. Constructing p-n heterojunctions may be a viable approach for enhancing the performance of the negative electrode, as it enhances the driving force for the built-in charge transfer and thereby it is beneficial for the charge transfer dynamics. Herein, we attempted to configure graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and LaMoO3 as a p-n heterojunction via one-step hydrothermal method, which shows outstanding capacitance performance. The specific capacitance of the LaMoG-3 (1396 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) was higher than the theoretical value for molybdenum trioxide (1256 F g−1). Moreover, the ASC is successfully assembled all-solid-state ASC with GQDs/Mn3O4 as the positive electrode and LaMoG-3 as the negative electrode. The as-fabricated ASC exhibited an ultra-high density of 235.8 Wh kg−1 and a power density of ~1000 W kg−1. One device can light the LED indicator for more than 11 min after only charging for 5 min. The above-mentioned results will open up promising research pathways for achieving excellent energy density and high power density.

head.ce:title: Boosted charge transfer in p-n heterojunctions LaMoO3/GQDs negative electrode for all-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Technology, Hohhot 010051, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Technology, Hohhot 010051, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The low energy density of asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) is limited by the negative electrodes; thus, to further increase the energy density of ASCs, it is necessary to optimize the design of the negative electrode and the device. Constructing p-n heterojunctions may be a viable approach for enhancing the performance of the negative electrode, as it enhances the driving force for the built-in charge transfer and thereby it is beneficial for the charge transfer dynamics. Herein, we attempted to configure graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and LaMoO3 as a p-n heterojunction via one-step hydrothermal method, which shows outstanding capacitance performance. The specific capacitance of the LaMoG-3 (1396 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) was higher than the theoretical value for molybdenum trioxide (1256 F g−1). Moreover, the ASC is successfully assembled all-solid-state ASC with GQDs/Mn3O4 as the positive electrode and LaMoG-3 as the negative electrode. The as-fabricated ASC exhibited an ultra-high density of 235.8 Wh kg−1 and a power density of ~1000 W kg−1. One device can light the LED indicator for more than 11 min after only charging for 5 min. The above-mentioned results will open up promising research pathways for achieving excellent energy density and high power density.

ce:section.ce:para: Asymmetric supercapacitors (ASCs) are at the forefront of this study and are considered a special type of device for the next generation of energy storage due to their outstanding power density and excellent cycle capability [1–4]. However, ASCs have low energy densities, which has restricted their potential application. To date, the reports on maximizing the energy density values of ASCs have been obtained by designing and improving the positive electrode [5–10]. Nevertheless, relatively litter effort has been made to design or improve the negative electrodes of ASCs [11]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for the design, synthesis and matching of ASC electrodes (especially negative electrodes) to construct ASCs with high energy densities.

Recently, extensive studies have been conducted on transition metal oxides (TMO) to find ASC negative electrodes with rich valence and a variety of electronic structures. Furthermore, of the various transition metal oxides, MoO3 is an electro-active two-dimensional (2D) layered material. It is alternately stacked along (010) under the action of weak van der Waals forces. These structures can be used for the insertion/removal of small ions, e.g., H+, K+ and Li+ (accommodate up to 1.5 Li/Mo), as well as reversible faraday reactions can occur and provide a short ion diffusion path [12,13]. However, most of the previous studies report that the MoO3 electrode exhibits sluggish faradic redox kinetics, a rapid decrease in cycle performance, and low capacitance due to the typical oxidation d0 with a band gap of 3.1 eV, resulting in poorer electrical conductivity (10−7 S cm−1) [14]. To improve the conductivity of MoO3, much of the research work has focused on converting MoO3 into a nanostructure that shortens the electron transport path or on conductive agent hybridization, for example, CNT [15], grapheme [16], or polypyrrole [17], to form the MoO3/carbon composite. Nevertheless, the complex preparation process and high cost of carbon nanotubes and graphene may limit their practical application [18,19]. Therefore, a convenient and efficient strategy to promote charge transfers in transition metal oxides to promote pseudocapacitor storage solutions with outstanding rate capability and long cycle life remains a challenge [20].

The fabrication of heterojunction structures is a valid way to enhance electrochemical activity [21–23]. Among the various surface engineering strategies, construction of p-n heterostructure at the surface or near-surface is an efficient solution to boost the charge transfer in TMOs [24]. Specifically, different from the interfacial restriction in composites, the p-n heterostructure engineers the charge transfer across the interface [25]. Moreover, the p-n heterojunctions enhances the surface reaction kinetics, as well as promote charge transfers induced by the internal electric fields formed on the hetero interface [26]. Wang et al. [27] designed a unique p-n heterostructure of Ni3S2/CdS, as CdS is an n-type semiconductor while Ni3S2 works as a p-type semiconductor. The Ni3S2@CdS nanostructures exhibited a high capacitance of 3.15 F cm−2 (2100 F g−1) at a current density of 2 mA cm−2. Gao et al. [21] constructed ZnO/Co3O4 as a p-n heterojunction as ZnO is an n-type semiconductor, whereas Co3O4 is a p-type one. The specific capacitance of the ZnO/Co3O4-450 (1135 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) was 1.4 times higher than that of Co3O4 (814 F g−1). Wang et al. [28] designed the p-n junction interface on the SnO@SnO2 skeleton, which resulted in a rapid conduction of electrons and ions between the electrolyte and active material.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are typically tens of nanometers in size [29] and are of interest because of their unique electrical conductivity, quantum confinement, as well as their tunable band gap [30]. In addition, studies have reported that GQDs can improve the poor conductivity of TMO to obtain excellent specific capacitance [31]. MoO3/GQDs (LaMoG) may be configured as a p-n heterojunction because GQDs are p-type semiconductors, and MoO3 is an n-type semiconductor. Therefore, the heterojunction structure of LaMoG nanobelts can be designed and fabricated to facilitate an electron and ion conduction between the electrolyte and the active material during supercapacitor (SC) charging/discharging and charge transfer and can protect the MoO3 from corrosion. Enhancing the SC properties to use a p-n heterojunction as the electrode material may be a viable method.

Based on the above discussion, we designed and synthesized the LaMoG nanostructures for the negative electrode of ASCs for the first time through a facile hydrothermal process. Furthermore, the LaMoG-3(when the GQDs mass is 300 mg) electrode shows a superior electrochemical performances that is higher than the theoretical value of molybdenum trioxide. In addition, it is well known that a larger voltage window can be obtained when selecting an electrode material with a large difference in the work function [32,33]. Therefore, the ASC constructed in this work is composed of the MoO3 negative electrode and Mn3O4 positive electrode. According to the work function of MoO3 (6.85 eV) and Mn3O4 (4.4 eV), it is expected that the difference in the work function of this combination is 2.45 eV [18]. Thus, to match the LaMoG-3 as the negative electrode, another high-performance pseudocapacitive material, i.e., GQDs/Mn3O4, is selected as the positive sample to construct a novel all-solid-state ASC with an operating voltage of 2 V (Scheme 1 ). The assembled LaMoG-3//GQDs/Mn3O4 all-solid-state ASC shows an ultra-high energy density and excellent cycle stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characterization of the LaMoG negative electrode
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the crystalline structures of the samples, XRD analyses were conducted and are shown in Fig. 1 a. The XRD patterns of the original LaMoO3 and LaMoG are well indexed with the α-MoO3 (JCPDS card No. 05-0508). Narrow with sharp diffraction peaks prove to be high crystallinity orthorhombic α-MoO3, and no impurity peaks are found, demonstrating that the MoO3 sample was of high purity. The high intensity diffraction peaks of (020), (040) and (060) in the LaMoO3 and LaMoG samples indicate that the nanobelts have highly anisotropic growth and better orientation. It is worth noting that compared to the XRD pattern of the original LaMoO3, the significant shift of the (020), (040) and (060) diffraction peaks of LaMoG-1, LaMoG-2 and LaMoG-3 to the low angle (Table S1) indicates that the interplanar spacing is increased [14], which could be attributed to the introduction of GQDs in the LaMoO3. However, with increasing the mass of the GQDs, the crystal structure of LaMoO3 has changed. As shown in Fig. S1, the (020), (040) and (060) diffraction peaks of the LaMoG-4 have undergone large shifts and new peaks have appeared, indicating that this may be due to excessive GQDs, which caused part of MoO3 to be reduced to monoclinic MoO2 (PDF 32-0671).

The morphology and microstructure of the prepared LaMoG nanobelts were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM), as well as transmission electron microscope (TEM). The LaMoG-3 microspheres consists entirely of uniform nanobelts, as shown in Fig. 1b and c; because the MoO3 structure was formed through the vander Waals interaction superimposed on the bimolecular Mo-O octahedron, the surface (010) is easily passivated by the growing species. Therefore, the reason for the formation of one-dimensional nanobelts is to suppress the formation of the (010) direction of MoO3 [12]. To further reveal the morphology and structure composition of the LaMoG-3 nanobelts, TEM characterization was performed. The TEM images in Fig. 1d further reveal that the nanobelts and the LaMoG-3 microspheres are composed of self-assembled nanobelts. Moreover, the morphology of ultralong MoO3 did not show significant change after HClO4 treatment, which will be beneficial for the assembling of free-standing microspheres [34]. LaMoG-3 with an average width of approximately 191 nm was prepared in the form of conventional nanobelts. However, in Fig. 1e, the width of nanobelt without GQDs is 287 nm, demonstrating that the GQDs have an effect on the growth of (0h0) of LaMoO3 nanobelts, which is consistent with XRD analysis. The SEM and TEM images in Fig. S2(a-i) reveal that the LaMoG-1, LaMoG-2, LaMoG-4 microspheres are composed of self-assembled nanobelts. Moreover, with the increase of GQDs, the widths of the nanobelts decrease, and when the amount of GQDs reaches 400 mg (LaMoG-4), the nanobelts become distorted and incomplete and the microspheres change from loose to aggregates, which is not conducive to the electrolyte ion transmission. The atomic percentages of the C, Mo, O and La elements in the as-prepared LaMoG-1, LaMoG-2, LaMoG-3 and LaMoG-4 samples were measured by EDX (Fig. S3). The detected atomic ratios of C:Mo are approximately 0.94, 1.25, 1.69 and 2.31 for LaMoG-1, LaMoG-2, LaMoG-3 and LaMoG-4, respectively (Table S2). Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP) was used to determine the actual content of La and Mo in the LaMoG samples, and the results are listed in Table S3. According to the ICP test, the La:Mo ratios are 0.23%, 0.25%, 0.22% and 0.24% in the active samples of LaMoG-1, LaMoG-2, LaMoG-3 and LaMoG-4, respectively. The sizes of the GQDs in LaMoG-3 are ≈3–4 nm, as shown in Fig. 1f. Since the GQD size is closely interrelated with the band gap structure, the different sizes of GQDs may influence the electrochemical performance differently. Compared to traditional carbon quantum dots, GQDs are usually smaller and have higher crystallinity [35]. GQDs have greater surface areas and more accessible edges, which effect the capacitance [29]. Lattice fringes can be observed on the HRTEM images (Fig. 1g). The 0.38 nm spacing of the lattice fringes and the (001) plane corresponds to the orthorhombic system of the α-MoO3 phase [36]. In the inset, the lattice spacing of 0.34 nm is very close to that of graphite carbon (002), which indicates a smaller size and higher crystallinity, which is consistent with the previous reports about GQDs [30,37]. The existence of Mo, O, C as well as La elements were confirmed in the LaMoG-3 nanocomposite (Fig. 1h) through EDS mapping images.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to further characterize the surface electronic state and chemical composition of the LaMoG nanobelts. The XPS spectra confirmed the co-existence of Mo, O C and La elements in the LaMoG nanocomposite (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S4), which was consistent with the EDS mapping analysis results. The Mo 3d core-level XPS spectrum is very suitable for the high-intensity Mo6+ doublet, as well as the low-intensity Mo5+ doublet, for the primitive MoO3, LaMoO3 and LaMoG samples, respectively(Fig. 2b and Fig. S4) [38]. The bimodal peak concentrations at 233.1 and 236.3 eV of pure MoO3 can match with the Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 orbital electrons of Mo6+, respectively, and well protocols with α-MoO3 [39]. Meanwhile, the Mo 3d spectra of LaMoO3 and LaMoG are shown in Fig. 2b and Fig. S4. Compared with pure MoO3, the peak positions of the Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 of the LaMoO3 and LaMoG shifted slightly due to the presence of oxygen vacancies [40]. However, the splitting widths of the Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 (ΔMo 3d = 3.1 or 3.2 eV) of all the samples (Table S4) agree well with the previous studies of α-MoO3 [41,42]. The atomic ratios of Mo5+/Mo6+ were calculated to be 0.395 and 0.43 in the pristine MoO3 and LaMoO3 nanobelts, respectively (Table S5). However, with the increase of GQDs, the atomic ratios of Mo5+/Mo6+ are greatly increased to 0.48, 0.56, 0.72 and 1.28 for the LaMoG-1, LaMoG-2, LaMoG-3 and LaMoG-4, respectively, indicating the partial reduction of MoO3. Therefore, after the introduction of La, the O-vacancy concentration has increased significantly; moreover, the introduction of GQD has not affected the defect. When the O2− ions are partially removed from MoO3, to keep the charge-neutrality of Mo6+, the material is reduced to Mo5+. Therefore, the formerly empty 4d band was partly possessed by the electrons. Moreover, as the O-vacancy concentration increases, the number of electrons occupied in the 4d band increases; additionally, the conductivity will be greatly enhanced. This could be demonstrated through ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (Fig. S5a and b). After the introduction of La, the absorbance of MoO3 increased from 424.7 nm to 444.4 nm. At the same time, the band gap is greatly reduced from 2.92 to 2.79 eV, proving that the electronic conductivity has been improved largely [43]. The O 1s XPS spectra of the MoO3, LaMoO3 and LaMoG-3 samples all show a strong peak concentrated at 530.8 eV (Fig. 2c), which is due to the existence of oxygen in MoO3 [39]. In addition, the O 1s core-level XPS spectrum of the original MoO3 showed a peak at 531.2 eV, which proves that chemisorbed oxygen exists on the surface of the material [12]. The high-resolution C 1s peaks of LaMoG (Fig. 2d) could be well-fitted to three groups to the sp2/sp3 carbon at 284.7 eV(CC/CC), alcoholic carbon at 285.7 eV(CO), as well as carbonyl carbon at 288.4 eV(CO) [44]. The main peak of CC/CC is derived from the GQD nanostructure, which provides excellent conductivity for LaMoG-3 nanocomposites, and ensures that the electronic charge and discharge quickly transfer. All the Raman spectra of the original MoO3, LaMoO3 as well as LaMoG-3 show three distinct Raman bands located at 680.7 (B2g/B3g , v as, OMoO stretch), 833 (Ag, v s, MoO stretch) and 1010.8 cm−1 (Ag, v as, MoO stretch, Fig. 2e), confirming the orthorhombic structure of α-MoO3 [18]. Especially, the spectrum of the LaMoG-3 sample exhibited an increase in Raman intensity compared to the MoO3 and LaMoO3 electrodes and had a slight shift. This phenomenon is caused by the introduction of GQDs, which enhances the local electric field due to the local surface plasmon resonance effect [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the LaMoG negative electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
We measured three electrodes in a 1 M Li2SO4 aqueous solution via cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), and evaluated the electrochemical performance of the electrodes as negative electrodes in SCs. Fig. 3 a displays the GCD curves of the synthesized electrode materials under a voltage window of −0.95 to −0.25 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at a current density of 2 A g−1. Impressively, it was found that the LaMoG-3 electrode had a longer discharge time, indicating that the capacitance of this electrode was significantly larger. Fig. 3b exhibits the CV curve of LaMoG-3 at different scanning rates of 1 to 80 mV s−1. The current density and CV integration area increase with the increase of the scanning rate, and all the CV curves show an approximately rectangular shape at a series of scan rates, indicating that the hybrid material has ideal capacitance characteristics [46,47]. The electrochemical insertion of Li+ could be explained by the following reaction:

where x is the mole number of inserted Li+.

In addition, the symmetric GCD curves also proved the prominent capacitive performance. Fig. 3c characterizes the GCD curves of the LaMoO3 sample at the current densities from 1 to 10 A g−1. Fig. 3d exhibited the C sp values of electrode materials at the current densities. After calculation, at 2 A g−1, the specific capacitance of the LaMoG-3 sample is 938 F g−1, as well as being approximately 2.8, 2.2, 1.7 and 2.9 times higher than those of the LaMoO3 (339.9 F g−1), LaMoG-1 (435.4 F g−1), LaMoG-2 (551.1 F g−1) as well as the LaMoG-4 (325.7 F g−1) samples, respectively, which was far higher than most of the previously reported MoO3-based samples (Table S6).

The total stored charge consists of the following three parts: the Faraday contribution of the lithium ion intercalation process, the Faraday contribution of the Faraday process, as well as the non-faradaic contribution of the double layer effect. Then, the processed CV current (i) is processed at different scan rates (v) [48,49] based on the following Eq. (1)
(1)

av
b

Among them, the i and v redraw the power-law relationship, which calculates the contribution factor of the capacitive current (b). For the LaMoG-3 electrode (Fig. 3e), when the peak potential is −0.5 V and the b value is 0.5, proving the current mainly comes from the lithium ion embedding (diffusion controlled). When the b-value is 1, the signifying contributions come from the fast near surface activities (capacitance controlled), such as the fast surface redox reactions [50,51]. When the potential is above or below-0.5 V, the b-value change between is 0.5 to 1, indicating a “transition” area between that which is diffusion controlled and that which is capacitance controlled, though a clear boundary is not easy to define [51].

Careful consideration of the CV scan rate correlation can quantitatively distinguish the contribution of capacitance to the current response. To study the reaction kinetics of the electrode, the dependence of the peak current on the scanning rate was analyzed, and the current response at a fixed potential is represented as the composition of two independent parts, i.e., the surface capacitance (k1v) and the diffusion control (k2v1/2 ) process, and the formula is as follows [52]:

where i(V), k 1 ν as well as k 2 ν1/2 indicate the corresponding total current at the fixed potential V, the current caused by the surface capacitance effects, as well as the current caused by diffusion controlled Li+ insertion process, respectively. The scan rate dependence of the current is plotted according to Eq. (3) (In Fig. 3f). The linear behavior can determine k 1 and k 2 from the slope of the line and the intercept of the y-axis at each fixed potential, respectively. Through this procedure, we can quantify the currents generated by Li+ insertion and the capacitance processes. Fig. 3g shows the CV curve at 10 mV s−1, where the surface capacitance effects (k1v, shaded region) are separated from the total current (k1v + k2v1/2 ).

Fig. 3h shows the contribution of the diffusion control and surface capacitance of the total stored charge of the LaMoG-3 sample at different scan rates. Hence, when the scan rate is increased from 10 to 50 mV s−1, the contribution of the surface capacitance to the total capacitance increases from 54% to 81.9%. As the scan rate increases, the diffusion control diminishes, causing the total capacitance to decrease at a high scan rate. Therefore, we could conclude that for the LaMoG-3 electrode, most of the capacity is due to the surface capacitive effects. Fig. 3i shows a typical charge/discharge test to evaluate the LaMoG-3 electrode stability of the design via performing 8000 charge/discharge cycles at 5 A g−1; the capacitance retention rate reached 99.8% and the morphology stability after the cycles showed no obvious change (Fig. S6).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is used to understand the effect of introducing La and GQDs into MoO3 on the electronic conductivity behavior of the electrode. Fig. S7a shows the Nyquist plots of the MoO3, LaMoO3 and all the LaMoG samples, which show semicircles in the high frequency region and straight lines in the low frequency region. Comparing MoO3 and LaMoO3, it can be seen that LaMoO3 shows a large linear slope in the low-frequency region, indicating that the introduction of La increases the electrolyte diffusion rate. In addition, after the introduction of GQDs, the linear slopes of the LaMoG-1, LaMoG-2 and LaMoG-3 are all greater than that of LaMoO3, and LaMoG-3 shows the largest linear slope in the low frequency region, showing that the electrolyte diffusion rate is the fastest among all the electrodes. It is worth noting that if too many GQDs (LaMoG-4) are introduced, the charge transfer rate of LaMoG-4 is worse than that of MoO3. This is because the excess GQDs affect the crystal structure of the MoO3, which is consistent with the XRD results. The inset is an equivalent circuit for matching the fitted spectrum. The equivalent circuit consisted of the solution resistance R s, charge-transfer resistance R ct, interfacial diffusive resistance (Warburg impedance), which is designated as “W”, double layer capacitor C dl, as well as the faradic pseudo-capacitor C ps [53]. In Fig. S7b, the measured EIS of the LaMoG-based materials were fitted by an equivalent circuit. Fig. S7c is the Bode phase plot of all samples. Contrasted with the Bode phase plot of MoO3 and LaMoO3, it can be seen that the phase angle of MoO3 is 50° and the phase angle of LaMoO3 is 59°. Moreover, after the introduction of GQDs, the phase angles of LaMoG-1, LaMoG-2 and LaMoG-3 are closer to 90° than is that of LaMoO3, and the phase angle of LaMoG-3 is the closest to 90°, elucidated fundamentally that the capacitive performance of LaMoG-3 is close to that of a perfect supercapacitor [54]. However, the phase angle of LaMoG-4 is closer to that of MoO3. Fig. S7d displays the frequency plot of the capacitance-dependent real (C′) of the all the samples. As the frequency increases, the C′ decreases because the Li+ solution has poor high frequency access capability in the deeper pores of the materials [3]. The capacitance drops sharply above 0.1 Hz and remains unchanged above 10 Hz. The lithium ion penetrates into the depth of the pores of the material at a low frequency, thereby entering more electrode surfaces, which helps to increase the capacitance value. It is difficult for Li+ to enter the deep pores of the electrode at higher frequencies, resulting in a sharp drop in capacitance. It is worth noting that after the introduction of the GQDs, the C′ values of the LaMoG-based samples are greater than those of pure MoO3 and LaMoO3. Among all the materials, the LaMoG-3 sample has the largest C′, which can reach 0.23 F. The peak of the imaginary part of the capacitor (C″) as a function of frequency in Fig. S7e indicates that the maximum capacitance (C″) of the four electrodes at frequency f0 represent the relaxation time (τ0 = 1/f0 ) [53]. The τ0 exhibits the minimum time demanded in discharging the energy from the capacitor [54]. According to this formula, the value of τ0 of LaMoG-3 is only 19 s (Fig. S8). In summary, the application of the LaMoG-3 electrode as the negative to construct SCs with high energy densities shows promise.

This high-performance electrochemical performance can be attributed to the following factors. Fig. 4 displays a probable mechanism of enhancing the capacitance. It is well known that the combination of different band-gap nanomaterials can form different heterogeneous structures, thereby improving the charge transport and surface reaction kinetics [25,26]. The internal electric field formed between the heterogeneous interfaces enhances the charge transfer. Moreover, the heterostructure provides sufficient channels and space to allow the ions and electrons to travel between the electrolyte and the surface of the active material, thus maintaining the excellent capacitance. LaMoO3 is an n-type semiconductor with a band gap of 2.79 eV and a p-type semiconductor of GQDs. The GQDs/LaMoO3 p-n heterojunction could generate an internal electric field at the heterointerfaces, which greatly accelerates the charge transfer rate of the electrode and improves the electrochemical performance of the electrode. During the charge process, the micro-domain of GQDs could attract more Li+, form a Li+ rich zone in the GQDs phase, and form a Li+ lean region in the LaMoO3 phase. A new electric field will be generated on the GQD interface to LaMoO3; due to the potential difference, it is beneficial to the migration of Li+ during charging [22]. During the discharge process, the direction of the electric field caused via the p-n heterojunction moves from the LaMoO3 to the GQDs. The charge accumulation layer at the interface is destroyed by the electric field, and the diffusion of Li+ in the bulk will becomes easier, which contributes to the insertion of Li+ [25,55]. Therefore, due to the strong synergy between the LaMoO3 and GQDs, the prepared electrode materials exhibit excellent specific capacitance and a brilliant rate capability with outstanding cycle performance when used as the negative electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characterization of the GQDs/Mn3O4 positive electrode
ce:section.ce:para: To suit the voltage window of the LaMoG-3 negative electrode well, the GQDs/Mn3O4 composite material was constructed as the positive electrode due to the difference between the maximum working functions of MoO3 and Mn3O4. The specific morphology and detailed nanostructure of the GQDs/Mn3O4 was evaluated via SEM and TEM (Fig. S9). Based on the SEM images of Fig. S9a, the nanosheet structure of the GQDs/Mn3O4 sample is exhibited. Many of the GQDs are uniformly distributed, providing evidence that a large amount of GQDs were deposited on the Mn3O4 sheet surfaces (Fig. S9b and c). These results that demonstrate the deposition of the GQDs on the surfaces of the Mn3O4 nanosheets did not cause changes in the morphologies of the nanosheets. In addition, the GQDs could provide excellent conductivity for the GQDs/Mn3O4 nanocomposite, ensuring fast electron transfer within the process of charge/discharge. Moreover, the EDS (Fig. S9d) further reveals the composition of the GQDs/Mn3O4 and the presence of Mn, O and C. In the XRD spectra of the samples, characteristic diffraction peaks of GQDs/Mn3O4 were observed, as shown in Fig. S9e. The index peaks characteristics of the sample agree well with the reference values (JCPDS No. 24-0734) [3]. Fig. S9f shows the XPS results of the prepared GQDs/Mn3O4, in which the XPS peaks of Mn 2p, O1s and C1s are shown. Two Mn 2p peaks were found, as follows (Fig. S9g): the binding energy of one Mn 2p1/2 peak was 654 eV, as well as the binding energy of the other Mn 2p3/2 peak was 642.2 eV. The binding energy difference of 11.8 eV proved to be Mn3O4 [56]. In addition, the XPS spectrum of Mn 2p3/2 could be separated into two peaks, i.e., MnO and Mn2O3, at 641.7 and 643.0 eV, respectively. The detailed O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S9h) shows three fitted peaks, as follows: the binding energy of the Mn-O-Mn in MnO is 530 eV, the binding energy of the hydroxyl group Mn-O-H is 531.3 eV, and the binding energy of the adsorbed water H-O-H is 533.2 eV. Additionally, the high-intensity C 1s pattern from GQDs/Mn3O4 (Fig. S9i) could be identified as the three peaks corresponding to sp2/sp3 carbon (C = C/C-C) at 284.8 eV, alcoholic carbon (C-O) at 285.4 eV, as well as carbonyl carbon (C = O) at 288.0 eV [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the GQDs/Mn3O4 positive electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 displays the electrochemical performance characteristics of the Mn3O4 and GQDs/Mn3O4 positive electrodes. Comparison of the CV curves of the Mn3O4 and GQDs/Mn3O4 samples at a scan rate of 80 mV s−1. Impressively, the GQDs/Mn3O4 hybrid electrode CV curve has a larger closed area than do the other electrodes, indicating that the GQDs/Mn3O4 shows enhanced electrochemical performance (Fig. 5a). Comparing the GCD curves of the two samples at the same current density, it was found that the GQDs/Mn3O4 electrode had a longer discharge time (Fig. S10), which indicates that the capacitance of this composite was notably larger. The typical CV curves of the GQDs/Mn3O4 sample is displayed at sweep rates from 10 to 200 mV s−1 (Fig. 5b). All the CV curves displayed quasi-rectangles shapes, especially at low scan rates due to the apparent pseudo-capacitance characteristics of the electrode, namely, the Faraday reaction of GQDs/Mn3O4 [57,58] As the scan rate increases, the shape of the cyclic voltammogram changes from rectangular shape to quasi-rectangular shape, which may be caused by the incomplete adsorption of electrolytic ions and/or those polarized on the surface of the GQDs/Mn3O4 [57] Fig. 5c shows the typical GCD curves for the GQDs/Mn3O4 sample from 1 to 10 A g−1of different currents over voltage from −0.2 to1.1 V. The nearly symmetrical with nonlinear patterns associated with the GCD curves, further confirm the presence of the Faraday redox reaction, indicating that the active material has a strong pseudocapacitive behavior. To evaluate the practical application, Fig. 5d shows the GCD characteristics and corresponding specific capacitance values at different current densities. Moreover, the GQDs/Mn3O4 displayed the high specific capacitance values of 550.4, 491.5, 467.1, 419.8, 367.3, 324.3, 302.3, 265.6 and 244.6 F g−1 at the various discharge current densities of 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 5, 8, 10, 15 as well as 20 A g−1, respectively, which was far higher than most formerly described Mn3O4-based samples (Table S7). To further understand the superiority of the electrochemical performance of the Mn3O4 electrode over the GQDs/Mn3O4 composite, an electrochemical impedance spectroscopy study was performed. In Fig. 5e, the Rct of the GQDs/Mn3O4 composite was much smaller than that of the Mn3O4 sample, indicating that the electrochemical reaction efficiency of GQDs/Mn3O4 was higher than that of the Mn3O4 sample. The lower R s is 1.9 Ω for the GQDs/Mn3O4 electrode but is 3.6 Ω for the GQDs/Mn3O4 electrode. Moreover, compared with the Mn3O4 sample, the GQDs/Mn3O4 composite has a more desirable straight line in the low frequency region, so it has a lower diffusion resistance. Therefore, the low resistance and fast charge transfer contribute to the large capacitance and good rate capability of the GQDs/Mn3O4 samples. As depicted in Fig. 5f, the phase angle of the GQDs/Mn3O4 in the Bode plot is approximately 65.1°, which indicates that the capacitance performance is close to that of the desirable capacitor [53]

To better understand the relationship between the increase of capacitance and the introduction of GQD into Mn3O4, we proposed a mechanism hypothesis (Fig. S11). The GQD band gap increases as the GQD size decreases. This is similar with the previously reported relationship Eg (eV) = 1.68 eV nm/L, where L is the size and Eg is the energy gap [59] According to above results, the GQDs are approximately 3–4 nm and the calculated band gap is approximately 0.6 eV. In addition, the work function of the GQD is related to its size, and work function of 3 nm is approximately 5.2 eV [60,61] In general, due to the different work functions of the two samples, a built-in electric field is created at the interface between them [62,63] The work functions of the GQDs and Mn3O4 in the GQDs/Mn3O4 are 5.2 and 4.4 eV, respectively [18], meaning that when the two materials are in contact, free electrons will be injected from the Mn3O4 at the interface to the GQDs until the Fermi level is aligned. When the Mn3O4 and GQDs are contacted by Mn-O-C covalent bond and form a heterostructure, the Mn3O4 has a positive charge near the interface, and GQDs are negatively charged. Moreover, due to the small lateral dimensions of the GQDs, the built-in electric field cannot be completely shielded, which allows free electrons to accumulate near the surface of the GQDs [29]. Clearly, the free electrons that accumulate on the edge of the GQD cause additional electrostatic attraction of the Li+ to increase the utilization of the cations, causing a synergistic effect of enhanced specific capacitance [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of the LaMoG-3//GQDs/Mn3O4 all-solid-state ASCs
ce:section.ce:para: The main goal of this work is to construct an all-solid-state ASC device with excellent specific capacitance and a wide operating voltage to meet the practical application needs of high-power supercapacitors. Thus, to further assess the practical application of LaMoG-3 and GQDs/Mn3O4 for lectrochemical energy storage, an all-solid-state ASC was designed in Fig. 6 a by employing GQDs/Mn3O4 and LaMoG-3 as the positive and negative electrodes. The electrochemical properties of LaMoG-3 as well as GQDs/Mn3O4 were evaluated regarding their applicability in energy storage devices. Therefore, the CV curves of the negative as well as positive samples were tested at 30 mV s−1 to assess the device’s working voltage window (Fig. S12). To demonstrate an ability to reconfigure the electrolyte system for higher energy applications, the PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte instead of an aqueous Li2SO4 electrolyte was used. Fig. S13 is a comparison of the CV curves of the Li2SO4 aqueous electrolyte and PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte at a scan rate of 200 mV s−1. Impressively, the closed area of the CV curve of the PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte is higher than that of the Li2SO4 electrolyte, indicating that the PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte shows a higher energy density. The GCD curves of the Li2SO4 and PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte further confirms this point (Fig. S14). Fig. 6b displays the CV curve of the device at difference scan rates with a 2 V voltage window. The CV curve reveals a similar rectangular shape at all scan rates, and even if the scan rate is increased to 200 mV s−1, the shape of the CV curve remains the same without any distortion, indicating that the ASC device has a high I-V response with excellent capacitance performance. This characteristic is consistent with the charge/discharge performances at various current densities (Fig. 6c), whereby a near linear GCD curve is recorded. The highest specific capacitance of 424.5 F g−1 was recorded as t at a current density of 1 A g−1, according to the GCD. The C sp values of the Li2SO4 aqueous electrolyte and PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte at different current densities are displayed in Fig. 6d. The electrochemical performance of the solid electrolyte is better than that of the water electrolyte of the ASC. To test the ion mobility and resistance of the device, the EIS diagrams of the Li2SO4 aqueous electrolyte and PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte devices are composed of a straight line in the high frequency region, as well as a semicircle in the low frequency region (Fig. 6e). The diameter of the semi-circle for the PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte is smaller than that of the Li2SO4 aqueous electrolyte device, and the slope of the line for the PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte device is larger than that of the Li2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. Moreover, the phase angle of the PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte at the Bode plot is closer to 90° than that of the Li2SO4 aqueous electrolyte (Fig. S15). Fig. S16 shows the frequency plots of the C′ components of the capacitance of the two device. The C′ value of the PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte device is higher than that of the Li2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. Similarly, a lower value of τ0 of the PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte device signifies that the surfaces of the electrode and electrolyte can access each other quickly (Fig. 6f). To detect the practical application of the obtained all-solid-state ASC device, Fig. 6g shows a typical charge/discharge test to evaluate the all-solid-state ASC stability of the design via performing 8000 cycles of charge/discharge at 5 A g−1. In the recommended voltage window of 2 V, the capacitance retention rate reaches 95.9% of its initial capacitance. Throughout the cyclic performances, the all-solid-state ASC maintains a high coulombic efficiency of approximately 82.7%, demonstrating a highly reversible electrochemical process. Overall, the LaMoG-3//GQDs/Mn3O4 all-solid-state ASC device had high specific capacitance and good cycling stability. Fig. 6h presented the Ragone plot of the all-solid-state ASC, and the LaMoG-3//GQDs/Mn3O4 supercapacitor exhibited the highest energy density of 235.8 Wh kg−1 with the power density of 1000 W kg−1. At a power density of 1000 W kg−1, the aqueous ASC had an energy density of 155.1 Wh kg−1, as shown in Fig. S17. The energy and power density recorded in this work exceed those of most of the reported ASCs (Table S8) [64–72]. The actual application of this ASC as an LED power supply is shown in Fig. 6i. The device can discharge the blue round LED indicator for more than 11 min after charging for 5 min.

In conclusion, a well-designed synthetic route for the synthesis of a p-n heterojunction structured LaMoG-3 nanobelts was designed. The formed p-n heterojunction will form a built-in electric field at the heterointerface, which will markedly increase the charge transfer kinetics as well as enhance the electrochemical performance. Hence, due to the well-defined heterojunction structure between the LaMoO3 and GQDs, LaMoG-3 exhibits the highest capacitance and excellent cycle stability compared to the other prepared products. It is important to design 2 V wide voltage window all-solid-state ASCs using a GQDs/Mn3O4 positive electrode and a LaMoG-3 negative electrode. When the power density is1000 W kg−1, the LaMoG-3//GQDs/Mn3O4 ASC displays an outstanding energy density of 235.8 W h kg−1, as well as remarkable cycle stability (The capacitor retention can still reach more than 95%, after 8000 cycles of charge and discharge.). This special structural construction effectively enhances the performance of the supercapacitor electrode materials and offers a unique view for the common improvement of the energy densities and power densities.
